Hanabi is a benchmark for research on Dec-POMDP.
Hanabi is described as a cooperative game for 2-5 players.
In Hanabi, 8 hint tokens are used.
In Hanabi, 3 life tokens are used.
Hanabi is played with a deck of 50 cards.
Each card in the deck has a rank between 1 and 5.
Both hint tokens and life tokens are essential resources in the game, with players having 8 hint tokens and 3 life tokens at their disposal.
The deck consists of 50 cards, each with a rank between 1 and 5 and belonging to one of five colors.
In the 2-player variant, each player holds 5 cards, which they can see the partner's hands but not their own.
On their turn, a player can choose to play, discard, or give a hint.
The goal of the game is to play cards in the rank order for each color, earning points for correctly played cards.
The team loses a life token each time they make a mistake, highlighting the importance of managing life tokens.
The OT-OBL algorithm trains a Q-function under the OBL setting, making it less prone to out-of-distribution errors when evaluated with different partners.
The algorithm involves training a Q-function with an associated policy and belief model, representing the decision-making process.

The team earns and loses points based on their gameplay and completion of objectives.

The backbone of the training is R2D2, a distributed recurrent Q-learning method.
Multiple environment workers work in parallel to facilitate training of the models.
R2D2 is described as a distributed recurrent Q-learning method with parallel environment workers and centralized replay buffer and trainer, making it part of the backbone system.
The backbone system involves the Q-network, which is trained to minimize the TD-error between its Q-value and targets from fictitious transitions.

The OBL models involve LSTM rollouts into fictitious transitions at training time, showcasing a sequential relationship between the concepts.
In the evaluation section, both XP and SP are mentioned, where every team is evaluated on 5000 games for SP and XP scores and averaged over five seeds.
The self-play cross-play gap (SXG) metric is introduced to better reflect improvement in intra-AXP. It is computed as SXG = 100 · (SP −XP)
SXG is computed as SXG = 100 · (SP −XP) where SP is one of the components
SXG is computed as SXG = 100 · (SP −XP) where XP is one of the components
SXG acts as a proxy for the covariate shift as it compares the evaluated training distribution (SP) with the test distribution (XP). The higher the SXG, the larger the covariate shift.
OT-BL is able to consistently gather a valid sample during training (> 99.5% of the time), regardless of penalty. OT-BL mitigates the covariate shift experienced during OBL training.

OT-OBL for ad-hoc teamplay and human-AI coordination. This set of experiments aims to demonstrate the ability for OT-OBL to improve test time performance with only a small amount of data from test time agents.

OT-OBL for ad-hoc teamplay and human-AI coordination. This set of experiments aims to demonstrate the ability for OT-OBL to improve test time performance with only a small amount of data from test time agents.

These experiments address the ad-hoc teamplay and human-AI coordination settings where there are large covariate shifts. We utilize rank bot for the ad-hoc teamplay setting and clone bot, a bot trained via supervised learning on human data, as a proxy for human-AI coordination.
